results.title: Custom 1982 Honda CBX Super Sport at No Reserve
results.description: This 1982 Honda CBX Super Sport was purchased in separate parts by the seller and was custom-built with a Suzuki GSX-R1000 inverted fork, a Ducati 848 single-sided swingarm and Öhlins monoshock, Keihin FCR carburetors, Motogadget electronics, and a custom red and white paint scheme. Its 1,047cc DOHC inline-six was overhauled during the build and was further modified with an alternator conversion, Dyna ignition components, a DG six-into-one exhaust system, and braided oil hoses. The bike is further equipped with triple Brembo disc brakes, Brembo control levers, a custom solo seat, rear-set foot controls, LED lights, and a carbon-fiber front fender. This CBX is offered at no reserve with removed parts and a clean North Carolina title in the seller's name.

results.title: Olive Garden enters India, chasing a diner who wants more than just a meal
results.description: For four decades, Olive Garden has built a reputation around a simple promise: when you're here, you're family. Now, the American-Italian restaurant chain is betting that India wants in on that family gathering too. The brand has opened its first Indian outlet at World St. in Aerocity, New Delhi, marking its official entry into one of the world's fastest-growing dining markets. The launch comes through a partnership between Gourmet Investments Hospitality Group (GIHG) and Olive Garden's parent company, Darden Restaurants, which operates more than 2,100 restaurants globally. For Indian consumers, the launch adds another global restaurant brand to an increasingly crowded premium dining market. Yet Olive Garden arrives with a level of familiarity few restaurant chains can claim in the United States, where it has spent more than four decades building one of the country's largest casual dining businesses. "Olive Garden has been around for 44 years," says Brad Smith, president of international & franchising at Darden Restaurants. "In the United States, Darden operates over 2,100 restaurants, and more than 950 of those are Olive Gardens." Brad Smith, president of international & franchising at Darden Restaurants Yet bringing that formula to India requires more than shipping over recipes and signage. For Smith, India represents the intersection of several favourable trends. A young population, rising disposable incomes, and a growing appetite for organised dining experiences. "India has got a massively growing and young population," he says. "The upwardness of the middle class has huge opportunities for us." The optimism also reflects broader growth expectations for India's food services market. According to the India Food Services Report 2024 by the National Restaurant Association of India (NRAI), the sector is projected to grow at a compound annual growth rate of 8.1% between 2024 and 2028. More than a plate of pasta The company is positioning the brand not as another Italian restaurant but as a hospitality-first experience. In conversations with executives from both companies, the word "experience" surfaces almost as often as "food". Rohan Pewekar, chief executive officer of GIHG, argues that Olive Garden's biggest differentiator is not its menu but its service philosophy. Rohan Pewekar, chief executive officer of GIHG "What differentiates is the warmth of how the food is prepared, how the experience is delivered," he says. "We often talk about Olive Garden being like a second home." That idea has shaped everything from staff training to table service. According to Pewekar, Darden deployed 16 trainers to India for over two weeks ahead of launch, focusing not only on food preparation but also on recreating the service culture the brand is known for internationally. The goal, executives insist, is not simply unlimited servings. It is anticipating what guests need before they ask for it. If that sounds like a small distinction, Olive Garden believes it is precisely where its competitive advantage lies. An American icon with an Indian accent The food, meanwhile, undergoes substantial localisation. Darden follows what Smith describes as an "80-20" framework internationally, where roughly 80% of the core menu remains intact while the remaining 20% is adapted to local tastes and market realities. India, however, proves more complicated. While signature dishes such as Chicken Alfredo, Lasagna Classico and Chicken Parmigiana remain central to the offering, recipes have been reworked to accommodate local dietary preferences and restrictions. Beef has been removed entirely, vegetarian options have been expanded, and several dishes have been reformulated without sacrificing what Smith calls Olive Garden's core flavour profile. "Our breadsticks are the same as they've always been. Our Alfredo sauce is the same as it's always been," he says. "But we've adjusted dishes to fit into what Indian consumers want." The balancing act extends beyond the kitchen. Step inside the Aerocity outlet and the restaurant feels unmistakably Olive Garden, though not exactly like one found in Orlando or Chicago. Signature architectural cues such as arched booths, wine cabinets and familiar artwork remain intact, but colours and finishes have been adapted to better suit local sensibilities. A look inside Olive Garden’s latest India outlet "It's got to look and feel like an Olive Garden," Smith says. "But some of the colours are different because we felt they were more conducive here." The choice of Aerocity is equally deliberate. For a brand making its India debut, visibility matters almost as much as footfall. Located amid hotels, office spaces and a steady stream of international travellers, Worldmark offers exposure to multiple consumer groups at once, from business travellers to families and affluent urban diners. "When you bring a brand like Olive Garden to India, it has to be seen," Pewekar says. Betting on India's dining boom That visibility is only the beginning. GIHG plans to open nearly 10 Olive Garden restaurants across India over the next three years, with further expansion in Delhi expected before the brand moves into other major metros. The ambition reflects confidence not only in Olive Garden but in the broader trajectory of organised dining in India. Pewekar points to industry projections suggesting organised food service could grow significantly by the end of the decade, fuelled by rising incomes, increasing exposure to global brands and consumers seeking experiences that justify spending more time and money outside the home. The challenge, of course, is that almost every premium restaurant brand is chasing the same consumer. Olive Garden's wager is that while competitors may copy the pasta, the décor or even the breadsticks, replicating the feeling of being treated like family is considerably harder. The company now gets to find out whether that promise translates as effectively in New Delhi as it does in Florida.
